title: Treaty of 1801 and Annexation
focus: Details the Subsidiary Alliance under the Treaty of 1801 and the final annexation of Awadh in 1856 on grounds of maladministration.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 055]
The Treaty of 1801: End of Independence
Subsidiary Alliance
The Treaty of 1801 established a Subsidiary Alliance between the British East India Company and Nawab Saadat Ali Khan II, effectively making Awadh a British protectorate.
Territorial Losses
The Nawab had to cede about half his territory, including Rohilkhand and the Doab, to pay for British troops stationed in his domain.
Loss of Sovereignty
He had to significantly reduce his own army and accept British control over his foreign policy, marking the end of Awadh's independence.
This treaty effectively made the state of Awadh a vassal of the East India Company, although it continued to be part of the Mughal Empire in name until 1819.

[Page 056]
The Annexation of 1856
British Takeover
In 1856, the East India Company first moved its troops to the border, then annexed the state for alleged maladministration. Awadh was placed under a chief commissioner – Sir Henry Lawrence.
Wajid Ali Shah, the then Nawab, was imprisoned, then exiled by the East India Company to Calcutta.
